provenance.source_corpus: Amelioration of degraded soils under red pine plantations on the Oak Ridges Moraine, Ontario

source_spans.text: After reforestation, soil restoration was characterized by cessation of erosion, accelerated horizon development and differentiation, reduced soil bulk density, and increased fertility and acidification of the soil.

After reforestation, soil restoration was characterized by cessation of erosion, accelerated horizon development and differentiation, reduced soil bulk density, and increased fertility and acidification of the soil.

After reforestation, soil restoration was characterized by cessation of erosion, accelerated horizon development and differentiation, reduced soil bulk density, and increased fertility and acidification of the soil.

After reforestation, soil restoration was characterized by cessation of erosion, accelerated horizon development and differentiation, reduced soil bulk density, and increased fertility and acidification of the soil.

After reforestation, soil restoration was characterized by cessation of erosion, accelerated horizon development and differentiation, reduced soil bulk density, and increased fertility and acidification of the soil.

After reforestation, soil restoration was characterized by cessation of erosion, accelerated horizon development and differentiation, reduced soil bulk density, and increased fertility and acidification of the soil.
Soil organic C increased from 13.3 g kg–1 at the Abandoned stage to 20.9 g kg–1 in Mature Plantations (Fig. 4).
Soil organic C increased from 13.3 g kg–1 at the Abandoned stage to 20.9 g kg–1 in Mature Plantations (Fig. 4).

Extensive deforestation after early European settlement followed by cultivation and pasturing of the land resulted in widespread soil degradation during the late 1800s and early 1900s, leading to eventual abandonment of farmlands.

Extensive deforestation after early European settlement followed by cultivation and pasturing of the land resulted in widespread soil degradation during the late 1800s and early 1900s, leading to eventual abandonment of farmlands.

Soil degradation generally involved reduced fertility with profile simplification (haploidization) on non-eroded fallowed fields, and topsoil loss by wind erosion (deflation) on more exposed eroded fields.
